summary: The commenter supports modernization and the shift to FHIR-based interoperability while emphasizing that deregulation should not compromise patient safety, data accuracy, or privacy protections. They advocate for maintaining core security and interoperability standards even as outdated requirements are removed to reduce burden.
specific_provisions: removal of Consolidated CDA (C-CDA) creation certification criterion
removal or reduction of audit log requirements
removal of privacy and security certification requirements including authentication, encryption, audit logging
key_arguments: Removing outdated technical requirements can reduce administrative burden and allow focus on modern technologies.
Core functions supporting safe clinical decision-making must be preserved to avoid risks like delayed care or clinical errors.
Security safeguards such as audit logs, authentication, and encryption are foundational to patient privacy and trust.
Public health reporting simplification should not compromise reliability and completeness critical for disease detection.
recommendations: Preserve core interoperability and security functions that support safe and accurate clinical care.
Maintain consistent baseline security standards in certification despite reducing duplication with HIPAA.
Ensure clarity and stability in USCDI data element definitions to avoid confusion and errors.
Balance expanded automated and AI data access with guardrails to protect system stability and patient safety.
Retain reliability and completeness in public health reporting even if simplifying mechanisms.
notable_quote: Modernization and the move toward more flexible, FHIR-based interoperability is supported. At the same time, patient safety must remain the guiding principle.
